questions.question: Explain the difference between queue overflow and queue underflow. When do these conditions occur in array-based queue implementation?
keyPoints: Overflow: Queue is full, cannot enqueue more elements - occurs when rear == MAXSIZE-1
Underflow: Queue is empty, cannot dequeue - occurs when front > rear or front == -1
Overflow is a memory limitation issue
Underflow is attempting operation on empty queue

questions.question: What are the initial values of front and rear when creating an empty queue using array implementation? Explain why these values are used.
keyPoints: front = -1, rear = -1
Both pointers at -1 indicates no elements in queue
This distinguishes empty queue from queue with elements
After first enqueue, both become 0

questions.question: In array-based queue implementation, explain what happens to the front pointer during dequeue operation.
keyPoints: front pointer is incremented (front++)
This effectively removes element by moving logical start forward
No element shifting occurs - O(1) operation
When front > rear after increment, queue becomes empty and pointers should be reset

questions.question: Describe the 'false overflow' problem in linear queue implementation using arrays.
keyPoints: Occurs after many enqueue-dequeue cycles
front pointer advances but array positions before front become wasted
Even with free space at beginning, queue reports full when rear reaches end
Leads to inefficient memory utilization

questions.question: Write the algorithm/pseudocode for the isEmpty() operation in array-based queue implementation.
keyPoints: Check if front == -1 (queue never used)
Or check if front > rear (after some operations)
Return true if either condition met
Return false otherwise - queue has elements

questions.question: A queue is implemented using an array of size 10. Initially it is empty. Show the state of front and rear after performing: (i) 5 enqueue operations (ii) 3 dequeue operations (iii) 4 more enqueue operations.
keyPoints: (i) After 5 enqueues: front=0, rear=4
(ii) After 3 dequeues: front=3, rear=4
(iii) After 4 more enqueues: front=3, rear=8
Number of elements = rear-front+1 = 8-3+1 = 6 elements

questions.question: What is the time complexity of enqueue and dequeue operations in array-based queue implementation? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Both enqueue and dequeue are O(1)
Enqueue: Add at rear index, increment rear - single operation
Dequeue: Read element at front, increment front - single operation
No element shifting required unlike in other data structures

questions.question: Provide a complete algorithm for implementing a queue using arrays. Include initialization, enqueue, dequeue, and display operations with proper overflow and underflow checks.
keyPoints: Initialize: Set front = rear = -1
Enqueue: Check overflow (rear == MAXSIZE-1), increment rear, add element at rear
Dequeue: Check underflow (front > rear or front == -1), retrieve element at front, increment front, reset if empty
Display: Traverse from front to rear indices
Handle special case when dequeuing last element - reset both pointers
Space complexity O(n), Time complexity O(1) for all operations

questions.question: Compare linear queue and circular queue implementations using arrays. Explain how circular queue solves the limitations of linear queue.
keyPoints: Linear queue: rear keeps moving forward, wasted space after dequeues
Circular queue: wraps around using modulo arithmetic, reuses freed positions
Linear: overflow when rear==MAXSIZE-1, even with free space
Circular: can utilize entire array, only truly full when front==(rear+1)%MAXSIZE
Circular requires modulo operation for index wrapping
Both maintain O(1) for enqueue and dequeue
Circular queue solves false overflow problem

questions.question: Design and explain an array-based queue implementation for a restaurant waiting system. What data would the queue store? Write pseudocode for handling customer arrivals and departures. Discuss any limitations of this implementation.
keyPoints: Queue stores customer data (name, party size, phone, arrival time)
Enqueue when customer arrives - add to rear
Dequeue when table available - serve front customer
Use structure/record for customer information
Timestamp for arrival order
Limitations: fixed size (overflow possible), no priority for VIP, no waiting time estimation
Circular queue would be better for practical use
